did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The paper used an Artificial Neural Network (ANN) with a backpropagation method to classify skin cancer (melanomas) using the ISIC 2018 dataset. Feature extraction was performed using Discrete Cosine Transform (DCT), gradient transforms, and first-order statistics, which were then fed into the ANN for classification.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The paper presents original experimental work involving a proposed hybrid method combining DCT, gradient transforms, and first-order statistics for skin cancer classification using an Artificial Neural Network (ANN) on the ISIC 2018 dataset. It includes specific sections for 'Proposed Method', 'Mathematical Methods', 'Result of the Proposed Systems', and reports empirical metrics such as accuracy (85.44%, 76.07%) and Mean Squared Error (3.52×10^-4), indicating it is a primary research study rather than a review.

title: Hybrid transformation with 1’st order statistics for medical image analysis and classification
brief_summary: This study addresses the early detection of skin cancer, specifically melanomas, by proposing a hybrid feature extraction method combined with an Artificial Neural Network (ANN). The researchers utilized the ISIC 2018 dataset, applying pre-processing steps such as hair removal and normalization before extracting features via Discrete Cosine Transform (DCT), gradient transforms, and first-order statistics. The extracted features were fed into an ANN with two hidden layers of 20 nodes each, trained using a backpropagation method over 1000 epochs. The model achieved an accuracy of 85.44% for DCT sub-bands and 76.07% for gradient sub-band transforms, with a Mean Squared Error of 3.52×10^-4. The results demonstrate that combining DCT and gradient approaches significantly enhances classification performance and supports efficient skin cancer diagnosis.

lesion_types_studied.details: The study focuses on the detection and classification of melanomas (skin cancer) using the ISIC 2018 dataset. The abstract states the ANN was utilized to 'discover and index diverse melanomas'. The conclusion mentions research into identifying 'melanoma, a type of skin cancer'. Table 2 lists 7 tumor types with detection ratios, and Table 3 provides a confusion matrix for these 7 classes, which correspond to the classes in the ISIC 2018 dataset (typically including melanoma, nevus, BCC, SCC, AK, BKL, DF). The paper does not explicitly name the other 6 classes but refers to them collectively as 'various types of skin cancer' or 'tumor types'.

all_task_types.target: Seven different classes of skin cancer/tumor types from the ISIC 2018 dataset
all_task_types.details: The paper explicitly states the goal is 'skin cancer classification' and 'categorization'. The model uses an Artificial Neural Network (ANN) to classify images into 7 distinct tumor types (labeled 1-7 in Table 2 and Table 3). The results include accuracy metrics (85.44% for DCT, 76.07% for gradient) and a confusion matrix for multi-class classification. While Mean Squared Error (MSE) is reported as a training loss metric, the primary task is discrete class prediction, not continuous value regression.

datasets_used.name: ISIC 2018
datasets_used.description: A public dataset from the International Skin Imaging Collaboration (ISIC) 2018 challenge, consisting of dermoscopic images of skin lesions. The study utilized images from this dataset for skin cancer classification.

use.training: 70% of the data (7051 images) was used for training the Artificial Neural Network (ANN).
use.testing: 30% of the data (3022 images) was used for testing the classification performance.

statistical_methods_used.method: First-order statistics (Mean, Standard Deviation, Skewness, Fourth Moment/Kurtosis)
statistical_methods_used.purpose: Used for feature extraction to characterize image data and reduce the amount of information needed for ANN training.
Used to evaluate the performance of the ANN training and measure the error between calculated and predicted values.
Used to evaluate the classification performance of the ANN for DCT sub-bands and gradient sub-band transforms.
Used to analyze the classification results, showing true positives, false positives, true negatives, and false negatives for the seven tumor types.

hypothesis_testing.interpretation: The paper did not report any formal hypothesis testing, p-values, or significance tests. Performance was evaluated using accuracy, Mean Squared Error (MSE), and confusion matrices.

ISIC 2018.experiment: Hybrid DCT + Gradient + First-Order Statistics with ANN

confusion_matrix_data.description: Confusion matrix for Hybrid method (DCT+Gradient+Contrast) across 7 tumor types

ISIC 2018.notes: The paper reports that combining gradient and DCT approaches produced a more successful method than using DCT sub-bands alone, which had lower accuracy rates and higher error detection. The hybrid system's MSE was lowered to 3.52×10^-4 after 1000 epochs. Table 1 in the text lists MSE for sub-band gradients as 12×10^-4 and sub-band DCT as 7×10^-4, while the abstract and conclusion specify the hybrid MSE as 3.52×10^-4.

architecture_details.layers: Input layer, 2 hidden layers, Output layer
feature_extraction_methods: Discrete Cosine Transform (DCT) on 8x8 pixel sub-bands
First-order statistics (Mean, Standard Deviation, Skewness, Fourth Moment/Kurtosis)
architecture_details.feature_vector_size: 15 features (3 color channels x 5 statistical features)

training_details.epochs: 1000 (for hybrid method); 200 iterations (for individual DCT/Gradient methods)

preprocessing_techniques: Noise removal using median filter to eliminate unwanted pixels such as hair from RGB images
Cropping to reduce white areas surrounding the cancer region and isolate the malignant component
Thinning to minimize the cross-sectional width of cancer strokes by eliminating foreground pixels
Resizing/Reduction of the cancer region to a 128×128 pixel resolution
Hair removal (elimination of unwanted hair pieces)
conclusions: The proposed hybrid method combining Discrete Cosine Transform (DCT), gradient transforms, and first-order statistics with an Artificial Neural Network (ANN) effectively classifies skin cancer, specifically melanomas, using the ISIC 2018 dataset.

The hybrid system achieved a Mean Squared Error (MSE) of 3.52×10^-4 after 1000 epochs, demonstrating improved performance compared to using DCT sub-bands alone, which had lower accuracy and higher error detection.
The study highlights that combining gradient and DCT approaches significantly increases the effectiveness of ANN training for cancer image categorization, delivering high accuracy and efficiency.
A key limitation acknowledged is the disparity in the number of images per class within the dataset, which makes it difficult to classify lesion image attributes accurately.
The work supports the potential of ANN in the early detection of skin cancer, aiding in efficient treatment and preventing advanced cases.
important_remarks: The study acknowledges a significant class imbalance in the ISIC 2018 dataset (e.g., Tumor type 5 has 6705 images while Tumor type 4 has only 115), which the authors note makes accurate classification of lesion attributes difficult.

The paper does not report formal statistical significance testing (e.g., p-values or confidence intervals); performance is evaluated solely using accuracy, Mean Squared Error (MSE), and confusion matrices.
The dataset split is fixed at 70% for training and 30% for testing, with no mention of cross-validation or external validation on independent datasets, limiting the generalizability of the results.

The preprocessing pipeline involves aggressive manual-like steps including hair removal, thinning, and cropping to isolate the cancer region, which may introduce bias if the algorithm relies heavily on these specific pre-processing artifacts rather than intrinsic lesion features.

The model architecture is a relatively simple Artificial Neural Network (ANN) with only two hidden layers of 20 nodes each, which may be considered weak compared to modern deep learning benchmarks for skin cancer classification.

The paper lists both ANN and CNN in the 'models tested' section but only provides detailed results and methodology for the ANN; the CNN is mentioned in the introduction as a comparative baseline from other literature but not evaluated in this study's results.

No data leakage risks are explicitly addressed, and the preprocessing steps (like hair removal) are applied before the train/test split, which could potentially leak information if the preprocessing parameters were tuned on the entire dataset.

The study focuses on multi-class classification of 7 tumor types but does not provide detailed per-class metrics (like precision, recall, or F1-score) beyond the detection ratio and confusion matrix, making it difficult to assess performance on minority classes.
